evidence: Vrijen warned against the 'death by a thousand cuts' of endless unpaid pilots. Instead, seek paid pilots with scoped changes and identify design partners willing to work with the product as-is.

source_display: AI as Brain Crutch for ADHD
evidence: Vrijen shared his personal interest in building AI tools as 'brain crutches' for ADHD—filling gaps between therapy, medication, and coaching.
Chris revealed he's been building his edtech product for almost a year, leveraging AI despite being a former special education teacher rather than a developer.
He defined product-market fit using the Supreme Court's obscenity standard: 'you know it when you see it'—when you have it, you don't think about it; when you don't, you obsess over it constantly.
Chris noted having 65,000 active email subscribers built over years—a significant distribution asset.
Vrijen emphasized that founder-led sales provides customer research, network building, and messaging refinement—critical activities for early-stage companies.
Vrijen invited Chris to join the Future of Higher Education group (6,000 members globally, ~1,000 in NYC, ~1,000 in Boston), where Vrijen serves as NYC co-lead.
The core concept is a 'marketplace of all possible futures' - allowing users to 'jump into' career simulations mapped to real jobs and companies.
His product enables users to fail safely and restart at the click of a button, removing consequences from learning. 'You can fail as many times as you want and then you can restart over.'
Max used the metaphor of a 'neopet' - anytime you train or deploy your AI requires 'energy,' which is where they monetize through usage-based pricing.

Careerspan pivoting to generate revenue through India recruiting partnership before targeting mid-year acquisition. The team consensus is to aim for a sale mid-year but spend the next few months generating revenue first, which Vrijen believes will massively improve their valuation.

Maximilian emphasized that his product creates portfolios first and assessments second, acknowledging that assessments are difficult to execute effectively. He criticized tools like HackerRank for not grounding results in actual business value.

Maximilian noted that visible links between individual work and the product/service being sold are increasingly essential in 2026. Engineers more than one step removed from the product were most vulnerable in layoffs.

Maximilian argued these roles [product owner, project manager] are 'done' in the current AI-enabled landscape, with many PM positions facing similar elimination unless they manage P&L and have product experience.
Vrijen described a self-fulfilling prophecy where increased hassle leads to corner-cutting, which then worsens outcomes and creates more hassle.
He introduced context engineering as the foundational practice: not prompting the AI directly, but cultivating the context it uses to generate desired outputs.

source_display: AI Productivity is a Mental Model Problem
evidence: Vrijen argued the core issue isn't a technology or tactics problem but a mental model problem—users collect techniques without understanding how they integrate into effective systems.

He outlined three strategic levels: Conversation (tactical), Environment (persistent context), Pipeline (external data integration) - mastering all three creates an emergent personal AI operating system.

Ben embraced the 'unhobbling' concept from the Anthropic/Claude team—recognizing the AI is capable of more than you initially realize. 'You can unhobble your AI a lot when you give it access to your files and data.'
Logan stated 'job security is dead and you need to be building career security for yourself... it has a lot to do with being findable and being visible.'
Logan shared his metacognitive patterns for working effectively with AI: consistently ask 'what else?' and 'delight me' - 'come up with like two to three things that would really delight me'

source_display: Plan Files as Future of Coding
evidence: Ben positioned this as the future of coding: planning systems through narrative structure rather than pure code alone—documenting AI conversations in plan files that mix prose and code in stepwise fashion.

Logan demonstrated deep analysis of 18 years of LinkedIn data using Claude Code, having exported 48 CSV files containing 5,000+ messages, connections, search history. Key insight: personal data is vastly underutilized—LinkedIn exports contain career-relevant intelligence hidden by platform UI limitations.
Logan documented 1,800% increase in inbound connections when increasing posting frequency, strong evidence for visibility strategies.

source_display: Inbound/Outbound Ratio as Social Capital Proxy
evidence: Logan identified inbound versus outbound connection ratio as a measure of professional standing—a social capital proxy.
Ben describes Vrijen as 'really amazing Zo user, kind of one of our power users' - they co-presented the AI productivity workshop together.
